coredata.dc:title: Mechanistic regulation of gram-scale synthesis of triple emission cyanobacteria-based carbon dots and visual ratiometric sensing applications
coredata.dc:creator: Yong, Chao
Lei, Ying
Liu, Yinghui
Li, Yu
Wang, Nan
Tong, Bihai
Tao, Lei
coredata.dc:description: Multi-peak emission is one of the most attractive properties of carbon dots (CDs). However, the mechanism of multi-peak emission is still not clearly explained, especially for biomass-derived CDs. Herein, we use cyanobacteria as carbon source, and prepare blue-light CDs and red-light CDs with triple emission via a facile microwave hydrothermal route. The CDs maintain stable and efficient fluorescence in solid state (QYs = 41.76%, 14.03%) with narrow half-peak widths of 74 and 30 nm. The experiment greatly improves the synthesis yield of CDs (40%), and expanding the experimental scale fivefold to achieve the gram-scale production. In-depth mechanism analysis revealed that the introduction of alkali can increase the self-doping content of N in CDs and convert pyrrolic N into graphitic N, further forming a hydroxyl shell on the surface of CDs. The synergistic effect of hydroxyl groups and graphitic N could adjust the energy gap, and triple-peaked emitting R-CDs are obtained. R-CDs are successfully applied to the visual ratiometric fluorescence sensor for Pb2+, which has a large linear range of 0–50 μM, 100–300 μM and a low lower detection limit of 0.85 μM. And R-CDs show a significant linear enhancement from pH 7 to 12,indicating R-CDs potential in the sensor field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Multi-peak emission
Gram-scale synthesis
Red fluorescence
Band-gap tuning
Visual ratiometric PL sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescent mechanism of the cyanobacteria-based CDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was jointly supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51974005), Open Research Project from the State key Laboratory of Refractories and Metallurgy (Grant No. G202001), University Synergy Innovation Program of Anhui Province (Grant No. GXXT-2020-007), as well as Youth Foundation of Anhui University of Technology (Grant No. QZ202210).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Simple preparation flowchart of B-CDs and R-CDs.
(a) UV–Vis spectra of B-CDs and R-CDs. (b), (c) Fluorescence spectra of B-CDs and R-CDs. (d), (e) Lifetime spectra of B-CDs and R-CDs.
TEM, HRTEM images and particle size distribution statistics (inset) of (a) B-CDs and (b) R-CDs. (c) Raman spectra (d) XRD spectra, (e) FT-IR spectra of B-CDs and R-CDs.
The full range XPS spectra (a) and high-resolution XPS C1s (b), N1s (c), and O1s (d) spectra.
Diagrams of the structure models (a) and energy level (b) of CDs.

(a) PL spectra of R-CDs upon addition of different concentrations of Pb2+ (0 ∼ 300 μm).(b), (c) Relationship between I468/I657 and concentration of Pb2+.(d) PL spectra of R-CDs at different pH. (e) Dependence ofI468/I657 of R-CDs on the pH values. (f). Changes in I468/I657 of R-CDs solution between pH 3 and 12.
XPS analyses of the N1s spectra of CDs.
XPS analyses of the C1s spectra of CDs.
XPS analyses of the O1s spectra of CDs.

head.ce:title: Mechanistic regulation of gram-scale synthesis of triple emission cyanobacteria-based carbon dots and visual ratiometric sensing applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Metallurgical Engineering, Anhui University of Technology, Ma’anshan 243032, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Metallurgical Engineering, Anhui University of Technology, Ma’anshan 243032, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials and Metallurgy, Guizhou University, Guiyang 550025, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials and Metallurgy, Guizhou University, Guiyang 550025, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Civil Engineering and Architecture, Anhui University of Technology, Ma’anshan 243032, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Energy, Hefei Comprehensive National Science Center, Hefei 230041, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The State Key Laboratory of Refractories and Metallurgy, Wuhan University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430081, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The State Key Laboratory of Refractories and Metallurgy
Wuhan University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The State Key Laboratory of Refractories and Metallurgy, Wuhan University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430081, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The introduction of alkali into the synthesis environment of cyanobacteria-based carbon dots can further aggravate the process of dehydration condensation and increase the self-doping content of N in CDs and convert pyrrolic N into graphitic N, further forming a hydroxyl shell on the surface of CDs. The synergistic effect of hydroxyl groups and graphitic nitrogen could adjust the energy gap, and triple-peaked emitting R-CDs are obtained.

Multi-peak emission is one of the most attractive properties of carbon dots (CDs). However, the mechanism of multi-peak emission is still not clearly explained, especially for biomass-derived CDs. Herein, we use cyanobacteria as carbon source, and prepare blue-light CDs and red-light CDs with triple emission via a facile microwave hydrothermal route. The CDs maintain stable and efficient fluorescence in solid state (QYs = 41.76%, 14.03%) with narrow half-peak widths of 74 and 30 nm. The experiment greatly improves the synthesis yield of CDs (40%), and expanding the experimental scale fivefold to achieve the gram-scale production. In-depth mechanism analysis revealed that the introduction of alkali can increase the self-doping content of N in CDs and convert pyrrolic N into graphitic N, further forming a hydroxyl shell on the surface of CDs. The synergistic effect of hydroxyl groups and graphitic N could adjust the energy gap, and triple-peaked emitting R-CDs are obtained. R-CDs are successfully applied to the visual ratiometric fluorescence sensor for Pb2+, which has a large linear range of 0–50 μM, 100–300 μM and a low lower detection limit of 0.85 μM. And R-CDs show a significant linear enhancement from pH 7 to 12,indicating R-CDs potential in the sensor field.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots（CDs）are emerging quasi-zero-dimensional materials, first reported in 2004, whose intrinsic properties are fluorescence [1]. Due to the good biocompatibility, rich physicochemical properties, unique optical properties, etc., CDs are considered to have a broad application prospect in many fields, such as medical imaging technology [2], electrochemical sensing [3], environmental monitoring [4], catalyst preparation [5], energy storage batteries [6], light-emitting diodes (LEDs) [7] and so on. Recently, various synthesis routes have been developed successively, such as electrochemical oxidation [8], laser ablation [9], solvothermal synthesis [10], pyrolysis [11] and microwave-assisted method [12], etc. However, most synthetic methods focu on the preparation of CDs with synthetic reagents and use large amounts of corrosive acids or toxic organic solvents, which not only increases the synthesis cost, but also causes secondary pollution. Instead, plentiful high-quality biomass carbon sources in nature are neglected, especially for the secondary use and harmless treatment of biowaste and biopollutants. Liu [13] et al. prepared the first grass-based CDs, initiating the trend of using biomass materials to prepare CDs. Subsequently, researchers successively developed a variety of biomass carbon sources. However, most CDs have extremely low synthetic and fluorescence quantum yields and are only monolithic in their blue light emission. And the aggregation-induced luminescence quenching usually occurs in the solid CDs, which severely restricts the further development of their applications [14].

Very few of the CDs reported so far have double or triple peaks emission, and most are single-peak emission, which limits the expansion of CDs in the field of polychromatic luminescence. For example, multi-peak emission spectra can usually cover a wide wavelength range and achieve a red-shift of the luminescence center, which is beneficial to avoid biological tissue background absorption and emission interference during bioimaging [15] and the manufacture of warm white LEDs [16]. Moreover, in metal ion sensing applications, it is difficult to quantitatively detect target analytes for fluorescent probes with only a single emission because of various factors unrelated to the analysis, such as instrument parameters, photobleaching, etc [17]. Ratiometric fluorescence sensors with two or more emission bands can greatly attenuate the interference of difficult-to-quantify factors such as temperature, polarity, pH, etc. by creating an internal standard [18], that is, a reference signal. However, the preparation of multi-peak emission CDs is not easy, especially for biomass-derived CDs. Most multi-peak emission CDs are often combined with organic dyes or semiconductor quantum dots, complicating the synthetic pathway while greatly weakening the advantages of CDs [19]. Others achieved double emission CDs by mixing and dispersing different emission band CDs in a specific matrix [15]. But because the characteristics of various CDs vary greatly, the mixed CDs suffer from problems such as self-absorption and fluorescence stability defects. Therefore, there is an urgent need to find a simple and low-cost route for the stable and efficient preparation of intrinsic multi-peak emission CDs.

Cyanobacterial blooms, a biological contaminant, are widely distributed around the world and pose a severe threat to local flora and fauna. With global warming and the intensification of water pollution, the frequent outbreaks of cyanobacterial blooms have become a global problem. In response to the issues raised above, we use cyanobacteria as carbon source to prepare intrinsic red-light CDs with triple emission (R-CDs) and have good fluorescence in solid state, and further expand the experimental scale to achieve gram-scale production of R-CDs, providing a new direction for the resourcefulness of cyanobacteria. A possible mechanism model for R-CDs is proposed to explain the fluorescence red-shift phenomenon. It is found that the synergistic effect of graphitic N and hydroxyl groups can further modulate the band gap of CDs, leading to long-wave emission. Finally, an attempt is made to use R-CDs as visual ratiometric fluorescent sensors for Pb2+ and pH, demonstrating R-CDs potential in sensing.

Cyanobacteria were salvaged from rivers in the countryside of Anhui, China. After drying at 105 ℃ for 12 h, it is crushed and sieved with a 60 mesh sieve to obtain cyanobacteria powder with uniform particle size. PbCl2, CrCl3, NaCl, KCl, ZnCl2, CaCl2, AlCl3, CoCl2, MgCl2, NiCl2, MnCl2, FeCl3, BaCl2, Hg(NO3)2 and NaOH (analytical grade) purchased from Shanghai Macklin. All aqueous solutions (≥18.25 MΩ·cm) are obtained with ultrapure water from a WP-RO-0B ultrapure water machine (Water purifier, China).

The morphology of the CDs was characterized by a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEM-2100). Raman spectra was performed with a Raman Spectrometer (HORIBA Scientific LabRAM HR Evolution, λex = 785 nm). X-ray diffraction patterns tested on D8ADVANCE. The surface functionalization of CDs was studied by fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra on Nicolet6700 spectrometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of CDs was carried on a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha electron spectrometer. UV–Vis spectra were measured on UV3600. Fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs were conducted using a PerkinElmer LS55 spectrometer. The absolute photoluminescence quantum yield (QYs) and fluorescence lifetimes of CDs was recorded on FLS980 and FLS1000 steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer.

Cyanobacteria powder (1.0 g) and NaOH (0.5 g) were uniformly dispersed into water (50 mL) and placed in the household microwave oven, afterward heated at 700 W power for 10 min. After cooling the hydrothermal carbonation products, the appropriate amount of deionized water was added for ultrasonic dispersion of 10 min and were centrifuged with 12,000 r/min centrifugation 15 min for three times. The CDs solution obtained by centrifugation was dialyzed with 500 Da dialysis bag for 24 h, then put into the freeze dryer for 50 h to obtain R-CDs. For comparison, the pure cyanobacteria aqueous solution was microwave heated for 10 min, and the above experimental steps were repeated to obtain blue-emitting CDs (B-CDs).

14 kinds of metal ion solutions were selected for the selectivity and anti-interference test toward Pb2+, such as Fe3+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Na+, K+, Hg2+. Each of the above metal ion solutions was mixed with R-CDs solution to ensure that the concentration of metal ions in each solution was 150 μM and the concentration of R-CDs was 0.2 mg/mL and placed for 20 min to ensure the equilibrium of the hybrid solvent system. The PL spectra were measured and the integral fluorescence intensities at 468 nm and 657 nm were calculated. To explore the anti-interference of R-CDs, 13 kinds of interfering ions such as Fe3+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Na+, K+, Hg2+ and target ions (Pb2+) were added into R-CDs together. The concentration of Pb2+ and interfering ion was kept the same. The PL spectra was measured when the mixed solution system was in equilibrium. To evaluate the sensitivity to Pb2+, a series of Pb2+ solutions with concentration gradients were mixed with R-CDs to ensure that the concentration of Pb2+ in the mixed solution was 10 nM, 50 nM, 100 nM, 500 nM, 1 μM, 5 μM, 10 μM, 20 μM, 30 μM, 40 μM, 50 μM, 100 μM, 150 μM, 200 μM, 250 μM, 300 μM, and the concentration of R-CDs was 0.2 mg/mL. When the mixed solution system was equilibrated, the change of PL spectra was tested and the ratiometric fluorescent probe was prepared.

250 μL R-CDs solution (2 mg/mL) was transferred into 9.75 mL different pH solution, and the mixed solution was placed in 20 min to ensure the equilibrium of the mixed solution system. The fluorescence spectra of the pH solutions were collected and integral intensities at 468 nm and 657 nm were calculated.

Fig. 1 shows a flow chart of the preparation of B-CDs and R-CDs. For complex biomass cyanobacterial feedstock, the effective red-shifting of the cyanobacterial CDs emission peak can be achieved with the addition of only a small amount of NaOH. The introduction of alkali significantly improves the synthesis yield of CDs (40%), and expanding the experimental scale fivefold to achieve the gram-scale production. Stable and efficient fluorescence can be maintainedin the solid state as shown in Figure. S1, the QYs of B-CDs and R-CDs are 41.76% and 14.03%.

Fig. 2 (a) is the UV–Vis spectra, B-CDs and R-CDs have a distinct peak at 256 nm and 271 nm, which belong to the π-π* transition of the sp2 hybrid aromatic ring [20]. R-CDs have two additional peaks at 411 nm and 656 nm that can be ascribed to the n-π* transitions of C = O and C = N [21,22], respectively. The red-shift of absorption spectra of R-CDs indicates that the band gap between the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) becomes shorter, and the wide absorption range also dramatically increases the possibility of multi-emission of CDs [7,23].

Optimization experiments are performed to obtain PL spectra of R-CDs with further NaOH additions, as shown in Figure. S2. Overall, in the case of 0.5 g NaOH, the intensity of the fluorescence emission peak is the best. Fig. 2(b)-(c) show the PL spectra of B-CDs and R-CDs. Under the excitation of 360 nm, the B-CDs have the highest fluorescence intensity at 447 nm with excitation-independent characteristics. The corresponding fluorescence spectra has a narrow FWHM of about 74 nm, indicating that the prepared CDs particles have a limited size distribution range. When the excitation wavelength is 400 nm, R-CDs show three emission peaks around 452 nm, 600 nm and 657 nm, and the corresponding FWHM is only 30 nm. Moreover, the PL peak of R-CDs at 657 nm is not related to excitation, which is due to the homogeneous particle size and uniform surface chemical composition [24,25]. This characteristic can avoid cell autofluorescence behavior and is suitable for many applications such as bioimaging, metal ion sensors and so on [26].

Fig. 2(d)-(e) show the fluorescence decay curve and its exponential fitting results, which indicate that the fluorescence lifetimes of the two CDs were 8.5683 ns and 4.2634 ns. Interestingly, the exponential fitting results of R-CDs includes the τ1 component related to the carbon nucleus and the τ2 component related to surface defect states, while the fitting result of B-CDs only contains the τ1 component. For R-CDs, the weight of component τ1 accounts for 26.02% of the total lifetime, and the weight of component τ2 accounts for 73.98% of the total lifetime, indicating that the radiative transitions associated with carbonaceous structure and surface defect states jointly affect the PL redshift of R-CDs. This further confirms that the red light emission of R-CDs is attributable to the carbonaceous structure and the defect states formed by surface dangling bonds.

Fig. 3 (a)-(b) are transmission electron microscopy (TEM) scanning images and high-resolution TEM images of B-CDs and R-CDs. The crystal plane spacings are 0.216 nm and 0.212 nm, which should be attributed to the (100) in-plane lattice of graphene [12]. The two CDs are almost spherical, uniformly distributed, and have good dispersion without any aggregation. The average sizes of the two CDs are 3.84 nm and 4.10 nm by statistical calculation, and the size distribution range is 2.5 ∼ 6.4 nm. These findings show that quantum size effect are not supposed to be the cause of the fluorescence difference.

Fig. 3(c) is the Raman spectra. The intensity ratios of ID/IG of B-CDs and R-CDs were 1.03 and 1.07, respectively. Therefore, the extent of graphitization is not the key factor of its fluorescence red-shift. Fig. 3(d) shows the XRD diffraction pattern. The diffraction pattern has a wide diffraction peak at about 2θ = 21°, indicating that B-CDs and R-CDs have no obvious crystallinity. This may be due to the amorphous structure caused by the disorder at the edge of the CDs or functional groups, as the incomplete carbonization of the cyanobacteria powder during the microwave heating process in a short period of time.

The surface functionalization of CDs is further studied by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (Fig. 3(e)). Here, the broad bands at 3411 cm−1 and 3077 cm−1 of the two CDs, corresponding to –OH and N–H stretching vibrations [27], indicating that a great quantity of hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs. The peaks at 1652 cm−1 and 1534 cm−1 are identified as C = N/C = O and amine II C–N bond stretching vibrations [28,29] and the characteristic peaks of primary amide and secondary amide appear at 1401 cm−1 and 1240 cm−1, corresponding to the stretch vibration of the amine III C–N bond [30,31]. The results indicate the formation of polyaromatic structures in their frameworks. It is noteworthy that the tensile vibrational strengths of C–N and C = N are higher relative to the B-CDs, indicating R-CDs have more graphitic nitrogen. As shown in Figure. S3, the zeta potentials of B-CDs and R-CDs are −19.8 and −44.1 mV, indicating that the R-CDs aqueous solution has excellent stability and resistance to aggregation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent mechanism of the cyanobacteria-based CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The luminescence mechanism of CDs closely linked to conjugated structure [35], surface state [36], size [37], etc. In this experiment, TEM, HR-TEM, XRD and Raman spectra confirmed that the two CDs have similar particle size, size distribution and nearly uniform levels of graphitization. Therefore, the multi-peak emission of R-CDs is not related to quantum size effects or sp2 conjugated domains. Based on FTIR and high-resolution C1s and O1s spectra, it is found that the alkaline environment exacerbated the dehydration condensation during the synthesis of CDs and a large number of hydroxyl groups are distributed on the surface of R-CDs. As a powerful electron-donating group, the –OH group can enhance the electron cloud density of CDs, raise the HOMO energy level, decrease the energy gap, and make the fluorescence red-shift [38]. However, in the existing research, the introduction of alkali has a limited effect on the fluorescence red-shift of CDs [39–41], and very few NaOH-modified N-doping CDs are capable of red-shifting to a red emission around 600 nm [38,42]. This corresponds to Figure S1, where only the emission peak intensity near 600 nm increases regularly with increasing sodium hydroxide content. This suggests that the emission peak near 657 nm should not be caused exclusively by the –OH groups. In the high-resolution N1s spectra, it is found that pyrrolic N plays a dominant role in B-CDs, while graphitic N plays a dominant role in R-CDs. This means that the introduction of alkali can increase the self-doping content of N in R-CDs, and further convert pyrrolic N to graphitic N. It has been demonstrated that pyrrolic N enhances the blue emission [43]. Graphitic N is an electron donating element, so it provides an extra electron to the π * orbital when substituting a carbon atom, while the excess electrons enter the unoccupied π * orbitals of the conjugated system, resulting in the creation of an intermediate band gap [32,33,44]. This is able to narrow the H-L band gap and leads to an extensive range of absorption and fluorescence red-shift. Therefore, the fluorescence red-shift of R-CDs at 657 nm results from the synergistic effect of hydroxyl groups and graphitic N.

To better illustrate the above point, a possible fluorescence mechanism model (Fig. 5 ) is offered to explain triple-peak emission of R-CDs based on the linear fitting equation ((αhv)2 = A (hv-E0)) of the Tauc plots [38,45] (Figure. S4). The PL peak at 452 nm originates from pyrrolic N with the band gap of 4.30 eV. The emission peak at 600 nm is derived from a great deal of –OH on the surface of R-CDs, which enhances the electron cloud density and reduces the band gap to 2.84 eV. The emission peak at 657 nm is due to the synergistic effect of hydroxyl groups and graphitic N, further decreasing the band gap to 1.83 eV. In summary, pyrrolic N, graphitic N and hydroxyl groups form intermediate band gaps in the R-CDs. After the CDs are excited, the electrons jump from the ground state to the excited state, most of the excited electrons are transferred to the excited states in these intermediate band gaps and then are converted back to the ground state by radiative transition, producing blue and red light fluorescence. B-CDs are dominated by pyrrolic N, the excited state formed by pyrrolic N (P-NS) radiates blue light. R-CDs are dominated by graphitic N and hydroxyl groups, producing graphitic N excited state (G-NS) and hydroxyl groups excited state (H-S), respectively. When UV light irradiates the R-CDs, a small fraction of excited electrons return to the ground state through P-NS to produce blue fluorescence. Most of the excited electrons jump to the H-S and G-NS, in which a few of them move back to the ground state through H-S and produce a faint emission peak around 600 nm in the PL spectra. However, most of the excited electrons come back to the ground state through G-NS due to the synergistic effect of graphitic N and hydroxyl groups, which can emit bright red light at 657 nm.

In this experiment, a visual ratiometric PL sensor was assembled for the detection of Pb2+ using I468/I657 as the output signal. The peak intensity of R-CDs at 452 nm remains constant with increasing Pb2+ concentration in a certain range showing in Fig. 6 (a). While the peak intensity at 600 nm weakened gradually. This PL quenching phenomenon could be attributed to the intense interaction of the functional groups on the surface of R-CDs with Pb2+. This could change the surface electron–hole recombination annihilation and ultimately lead to the quenching of R-CDs luminescence through photoelectron transfer [46,47]. We tested the UV absorption spectra of R-CDs before and after adding Pb2+ as shown in Figure S6(a). It can be found that the two UV absorption spectra almost overlap, and the UV absorption spectra of the Pb2+ solution (150 μM) does not show significant absorption peaks, which excludes the possibility of static quenching effect and inter filtration effect (IFE) [48,49]. Furthermore, the fluorescence lifetime of R-CDs after the addition of Pb2+ is shown in Figure S6(b), and it can be found that the fluorescence lifetime of R-CDs after the addition of Pb2+ increases from 4.2634 to 7.8359 and almost coincides with that of B-CDs, which corresponds to the fluorescence spectra of R-CDs after the addition of Pb2+ (the red light of R-CDs disappears and blue light is emitted). Thus, the possible reason for the quenching of R-CDs by Pb2+ is its preference to combine with the hydroxyl groups distributed on the surface of R-CDs through chelation or electrostatic interactions, significantly reducing the surface-confined holes and electrons [50]. The introduction of Pb2+ prolongs the life of carbon dots, which may result from its strong ability to capture electrons, interfering with electron-hole complexation between bands or defect centers and promoting non-radiative electron-hole recombination annihilation through electron transfer [51].

Fig. 6(b)-(c) show the relationship between the emission peak intensity ratio (I468/I657) and Pb2+ concentration. There is a good linear relationship between I468/I657 and Pb2+ concentration in the range of 0–50 μM and 100–300 μM, respectively. The linear equations are I468/I657 = 0.06604c + 0.80141 (R2 = 0.9917), I468/I657 = 6.60582–0.00599c (R2 = 0.9995), and the limit of detection (LOD) is 0.85 μM. The selectivity and anti-interference in Pb2+ fluorescent sensor are evaluated, as shown in Figure. S5(a)-(d). The addition of Pb2+ reduces the peak intensity at 657 nm by nearly 90% compared to free R-CDs, resulting in the rapid increase in the fluorescence ratio I468/I657. At the same time, other metal ions (Fe3+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Al3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Pb2+, Na+, K+, Hg2+) have no significant effect on I468/I657. It can be seen in Figure. S5(c)-(d) that compared with the fluorescence ratio I468/I657 of only Pb2+ quenched R-CDs, the fluorescence ratio I468/I657 after the addition of interfering cations did not change significantly, indicating that different interfering cations quenched R-CDs have little effect. The above experimental results show that R-CDs have good selectivity and anti-interference for Pb2+ detection.

The fluorescence emission peak at 657 nm gradually increases when the pH value is higher than 7 showing in Fig. 6(d), while the fluorescence intensity ratio I468/I657 decreases with increasing pH. This indicates that R-CDs have a strong pH dependence in the alkaline range. It may be attributed to protonation and deprotonation [52]. The linear equation (Fig. 6 (e)) is obtained by fitting the peaks intensitiy of R-CDs in the pH range of 7 ∼ 12: y = 1.82072–0.07883pH (R2 = 0.9995), which indicates that there is an excellent linear relationship between the ratio I468/I657 of R-CDs fluorescence intensity and pH. In addition, the PH of R-CDs solution was adjusted to switch back and forth between 3 and 12 for 5 times to detect the reversibility of the fluorescent PH probe, and its PL spectra were measured (Fig. 6(f)). The results show that the peak intensity is perfectly reversible in the range of pH from 3 to 12. The experimental results show that R-CDs are ideal sensors for pH detection under alkaline conditions with the advantages of good water solubility, high sensitivity, excellent stability, and good reversibility.

We use cyanobacteria as carbon source to prepare red-light CDs with triple emission by adding a small amount of NaOH, providing a new direction for the application of cyanobacteria. By introducing a small amount of alkali into the synthesis environment, the synthesis yield is increased (40%) and achieves the gram-scale production of CDs. At the same time, the stable and efficient fluorescence can be maintainedin the solid state. Experiments analysis shows that the introduction of alkali can increase the self-doping content of N in CDs, and convert pyrrolic N into graphitic N, further forming a hydroxyl shell on the surface of R-CDs. The synergistic interaction of hydroxyl groups and graphitic N could adjust the band gap of CDs, making the PL spectra red-shift to 657 nm. Finally, in the attempt to apply the obtained R-CDs to the visualizing ratiometric fluorescence sensors, it is found that R-CDs show high selectivity and sensitivity to Pb2+ with a low detection limit of 0.85 μM of linear range from 0 to 50 μM and 100–300 μM. And R-CDs can be used for pH sensing, showing a significant linear growth when pH is increased from 7 to 12, indicating R-CDs potential in the sensor field.

Chao Yong: Investigation, Formal analysis, Validation, Resources, Writing – original draft. Ying Lei: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Supervision, Resources, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yinghui Liu: Methodology, Investigation, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Yu Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Project administration, Resources, Writing – review & editing. Nan Wang: Investigation, Resources. Bihai Tong: Resources. Lei Tao: Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was jointly supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51974005), Open Research Project from the State key Laboratory of Refractories and Metallurgy (Grant No. G202001), University Synergy Innovation Program of Anhui Province (Grant No. GXXT-2020-007), as well as Youth Foundation of Anhui University of Technology (Grant No. QZ202210).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments
J. Am. Chem. Soc.
